title: Echoes of Rising Tuition in Students' Borrowing, Educational Attainment, and Homeownership in Post-Recession America

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This staff report examines how the 81% rise in public college tuition between 2001-2009 affected student borrowing, educational attainment, and homeownership. The research finds that tuition increases accounted for $1,628 (30%) of the increase in per-capita student debt among 24-year-olds, but did not meaningfully affect college enrollment or degree attainment. Instead, students absorbed costs through additional debt. The study estimates that these factors explain 11-35% of the 8-percentage-point decline in homeownership for 28-to-30-year-olds during 2007-2015.
key_points: State average public college tuition rose $3,843 (81%) between 2001-2009
Tuition increases accounted for $1,628 (approximately 30%) of per-capita student debt growth among 24-year-olds from 2003-2011
No meaningful response in college enrollment, years of schooling, or BA degree attainment rates to tuition changes
Student loan system allowed Americans to maintain human capital investments by accumulating more debt rather than forgoing schooling
Tuition and student debt increases explain 11-35% of the ~8 percentage point homeownership decline for 28-30-year-olds from 2007-2015
Authors: Zachary Bleemer, Meta Brown, Donghoon Lee, Katherine Strair, and Wilbert van der Klaauw (Staff Report No. 820)
why_it_matters: This research documents how the student loan system insulates human capital investment from tuition shocks but creates downstream wealth effects. For housing and consumer spending analysis, it quantifies the homeownership cost of rising education debt. However, it does not address financial market structure or traditional macro catalysts.
macro_context: The research examines long-term demographic trends linking rising education costs to household formation and housing market participation. While not a traditional macro catalyst, it provides context on structural factors affecting housing demand and consumer spending patterns among young adults.
market_structure_context: This research does not directly address financial market structure, trading mechanisms, or market infrastructure. It focuses on real economy dynamics—specifically how the student loan system affects household balance sheets and housing market participation.
important_numbers: 30% of per-capita student debt increase explained by tuition
11-35% of homeownership decline explained by tuition/debt factors

issues_found: Source content is primarily education/labor economics (student loans, tuition, homeownership demographics) rather than financial market structure research
Record accurately captures the source but the source itself has low relevance to stated archive focus areas (market structure, macro catalysts)
Published July 2017 (8.75 years old), exceeding 90-day threshold; archival value assessment needed
The TARGET metadata ('market structure') does not match actual source content

human_review.notes: Source content is factually accurate but presents a scope mismatch. The research is well-documented and credible (Federal Reserve Bank of New York staff report), but its subject matter—education economics, student loan absorption, and homeownership demographics—falls outside typical financial market structure/macro catalyst focus. Human judgment needed to determine if housing market effects from student debt qualify as market structure context for this archive, and whether the 2017 publication date still has sufficient archival value given the long-term nature of the findings.

notes: IRRELEVANT TO ARCHIVE SCOPE. This Federal Reserve staff report focuses on education economics and housing demographics, not market structure or macro catalysts. The TARGET field in metadata is incorrectly labeled as 'market structure'; the actual content concerns student loan debt, tuition absorption, and homeownership trends. Source is from July 2017 (8+ years old), low relevance to current financial market research needs. Available only in PDF format (48 pages / 915 kb).
